prompt:
Our ops scripts run on Linux CI runners and on the team's Macs, and every one of them carries its own crusty copy of the same three workarounds: GNU stat vs BSD stat, GNU sed -i vs BSD sed -i '', GNU date -d vs BSD date -r. I want that collapsed into one sourceable zsh 5.9 library, `portwrap.zsh`, in the repo root, and I have one hard design requirement learned the annoying way: DETECT THE TOOL, NEVER THE PLATFORM. Half our Macs have coreutils installed ahead of the system tools, so the OS name and the userland disagree all the time — any peeking at uname or $OSTYPE picks the wrong branch on exactly those machines. Ask the tool itself: `stat --version` answers on GNU and errors on BSD, same trick for sed, and `date -u -d @0 +%s` answers on GNU while BSD wants -r. Probe each of the three tools at most once per session, remember the answer, and give me `portwrap_reset` to forget all three cached answers (we re-exec after PATH edits sometimes).

Public surface, exact names:
- `stat_flavor`, `sed_flavor`, `date_flavor` — print `gnu` or `bsd` on stdout, exit 0. First call probes, later calls answer from the cache.
- `file_mtime_epoch <path>` — mtime as epoch seconds (GNU `stat -c %Y`, BSD `stat -f %m`).
- `file_size_bytes <path>` — size in bytes (`%s` vs `%z`).
- `sed_inplace <script> <file>` — edit in place with NO backup file left behind on either dialect; remember BSD's -i wants the empty suffix as its own argument.
- `date_from_epoch <epoch> [strftime-fmt]` — render an epoch in UTC always (force it; the caller's TZ must not leak in), default format `%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ`.
- `portwrap_reset` — clear the cached flavors, print nothing.

Contract details: sourcing the file prints nothing and must not disturb the caller's shell — write it the zsh way, `emulate -L zsh` at the top of every function, because our callers run with all sorts of setopt combinations and the library still has to behave. The three file helpers print the answer and exit 0; a path that isn't a regular file gets `portwrap: no such file: <path>` on stderr and return 1, stdout empty. A non-numeric epoch gets `portwrap: not an epoch: <value>` on stderr, return 1. Call the external tools with `command` so nobody's aliases interfere.

The acceptance harness is `zsh test_portwrap.zsh` from the repo root (don't edit it). Worth knowing before you start: it runs the GNU paths against the real userland on this box, and it pins the BSD paths by prepending a directory of stub tools that speak the BSD spellings — including a uname that reports the wrong OS on purpose, which is exactly why the probe-the-tool rule exists.
